q: A particle is projected with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. A damping force is acting on the particle which is proportional to the square of the distance from the origin i.e. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The distance at which the particle stops:
question: A particle is projected with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. A damping force is acting on the particle which is proportional to the square of the distance from the origin i.e. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The distance at which the particle stops:
solution:
F=-αx2

ma=-αx2

a=-αx2 m

vdvdx=-αmx2

∫v00vdv=∫0x-αmx2dx

v22v00=-αmx330x

-v022=-αmx33

x=3mv022α13

subject: Physics
q: Two projectiles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are thrown with initial velocities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal respectively. The ratio of their ranges respectively is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math>
question: Two projectiles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are thrown with initial velocities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal respectively. The ratio of their ranges respectively is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math>
solution:
The range R1 of the first projectile is given by

R1=u12sin2θ1g   ...(1)

The range R2 of the second projectile is given by

R2=u22sin2θ2g   ...(2)

Divide equation (1) by equation (2) and simplify to obtain the ratio of the ranges.

R1R2=u12sin2θ1gu22sin2θ2g= u12sin2θ1u22sin2θ2   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required ratio.

 R1R2=402×sin60°602×sin120°=49

subject: Physics
q: A person can throw a ball upto a maximum range of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. How high above the ground he can throw the same ball?
question: A person can throw a ball upto a maximum range of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. How high above the ground he can throw the same ball?
solution:
Horizontal range is maximum if the angle of projection is 45° and is given by Rmax=u2g

Here, Rmax=100 m

Then, 100=u2g  ⋯1

If the ball is thrown vertically upward, then the ball will attain the maximum height from the ground as Hmax=u22g

Hmax=1002=50 m

subject: Physics
q: A man in a car at location Q on a straight highway is moving with speed $\mathrm{v}$. He decides to reach a point $P$ in a field at a distance $d$ from highway (point $M$ ) as shown in the figure.Speed of the car in the field is half to that on the highway. What should be the distance RM, so that the time taken to reach $P$ is minimum?
question: A man in a car at location Q on a straight highway is moving with speed $\mathrm{v}$. He decides to reach a point $P$ in a field at a distance $d$ from highway (point $M$ ) as shown in the figure.Speed of the car in the field is half to that on the highway. What should be the distance RM, so that the time taken to reach $P$ is minimum?
solution:
Let the car turn of the highway at a distance ' $x$ ' from the point $\mathrm{M}$. So, $\mathrm{RM}=x$ And if speed of car in field is $v$, then time taken by the car to cover the distance $Q R=$ $Q M-x$ on the highway, $$ t_1=\frac{Q M-x}{2 v} $$ Time taken to travel the distance ' $R P^{\prime}$ in the field $$ t_2=\frac{\sqrt{d^2+x^2}}{v} $$ Total time elapsed to move the car from $Q$ to $P$ $$ t=t_1+t_2=\frac{Q M-x}{2 v}+\frac{\sqrt{d^2+x^2}}{v} $$
$$ \text { or } x=\frac{d}{\sqrt{2^2-1}}=\frac{d}{\sqrt{3}} $$

subject: Physics
q: A particle starts from the origin at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> with an initial velocity of <math><mn>3.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and moves in the <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi></math> plane with a constant acceleration <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>6.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math> coordinate of the particle at the instant when its <math><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math> coordinate is <math><mn>32</mn><mi></mi><mi>m</mi></math> is <math><mi>D</mi></math> meters. The value of <math><mi>D</mi></math> is:
question: A particle starts from the origin at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> with an initial velocity of <math><mn>3.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and moves in the <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi></math> plane with a constant acceleration <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>6.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math> coordinate of the particle at the instant when its <math><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math> coordinate is <math><mn>32</mn><mi></mi><mi>m</mi></math> is <math><mi>D</mi></math> meters. The value of <math><mi>D</mi></math> is:
solution:
Sy=uyt+12ayt2
32=0+12×4t2⇒t=4 s
Sx=uxt+12axt2
=3×4+12×6×16
=60 m

subject: Physics
q: A particle just clears a wall of height $b$ at distance $a$ and strikes the ground at a distance $c$ from the point of projection. The angle of projection is
question: A particle just clears a wall of height $b$ at distance $a$ and strikes the ground at a distance $c$ from the point of projection. The angle of projection is
solution: $a=(u \cos \alpha) t$ and $b=(u \sin \alpha) t-\frac{1}{2} g t^2$ $b=a \tan \alpha-\frac{1}{2} g \frac{a^2}{u^2 \cos ^2 \alpha}$ also, $c=\frac{u^2 \sin 2 \alpha}{g}$ $b=a \tan \alpha-\frac{a^2 g}{2}\left(\frac{\sin 2 \alpha}{c g}\right) \sec ^2 \alpha$ $b=a \tan \alpha-\frac{a^2}{2 c} 2 \tan \alpha$ $\Rightarrow\left(a-\frac{a^2}{c}\right) \tan \alpha=b$ $\tan \alpha=\frac{b c}{a(c-a)}$.

q: A player kicks a football with an initial speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from the ground. solution:
θ=45°

H=u2sin2θ2g=(25)2×122×10=1258 m

and time t=T2

=usinθg=251210=25102=522s

q: If the radius of curvature of the path of two particles of same mass are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math>, then in order to have constant centripetal force, their velocities will be in the ratio of:
question: If the radius of curvature of the path of two particles of same mass are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math>, then in order to have constant centripetal force, their velocities will be in the ratio of:
solution:
Given m1=m2 and r1r2=34

As centripetal force is given by F=mv2r, in order to have the same centripetal force, we can write

F1=F2⇒m1v12r1=m2v22r2⇒v1v2=r1r2=32

subject: Physics
q: An object is projected in the air with initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math>. The projectile motion is such that the horizontal range <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, is maximum. Another object is projected in the air with a horizontal range half of the range of first object. The initial velocity remains same in both the case. The value of the angle of projection, at which the second object is projected, will be _____  degree.
question: An object is projected in the air with initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math>. The projectile motion is such that the horizontal range <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, is maximum. Another object is projected in the air with a horizontal range half of the range of first object. The initial velocity remains same in both the case. The value of the angle of projection, at which the second object is projected, will be _____  degree.
solution:
The maximum range is given by, Rmax=u2sin245°g=u2 g.

For the range to be half of the maximum range,

Rmax2=u22 g=u2sin2θg

⇒sin2θ=12

⇒2θ=30°or 2θ=150°

⇒θ=15°,75°

Therefore, answer is 15 or 75

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving with velocity $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{K}(\mathrm{y} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{x} \hat{\mathrm{j}})$, where $\mathrm{K}$ is a constant. The general equation for its path is
question: A particle is moving with velocity $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{K}(\mathrm{y} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{x} \hat{\mathrm{j}})$, where $\mathrm{K}$ is a constant. The general equation for its path is
solution:
$\vec{v}=K y \hat{i}+K x \hat{j}$
$\frac{d x}{d t}=K y, \quad \frac{d y}{d t}=K x$
$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \times \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{K x}{K y}$
$y d y=x d x$
$y^2=x^2+c$

q: The water is filled up to a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in a tank having vertical sidewalls. A hole is made in one of the walls at a depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> below the water level. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> for which the emerging stream of water strikes the ground at the maximum range is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.

solution:
R=2gh×(12-h)×2 g

4 h(12-h)=R

For maximum R

dRdh=0

⇒h=6 m

question: A projectile is given an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, where <math> <mrow> <mover accent="true"> <mtext>i</mtext> <mo>^</mo> </mover> </mrow> </math> is along the ground and <math> <mrow> <mover accent="true"> <mtext>j</mtext> <mo>^</mo> </mover> </mrow> </math> is along the vertical upward. solution:
v → = i ^ + 2 j ^

⇒ x = t    ..... (i)

y = 2 t - 1 2 1 0 t 2    ..... (ii)

From (i) and (ii)

y = 2 x - 5 x 2

q: Two particles are projected from the same point with the same speed u such that they have the same range R, but different maximum heights, <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Which of the following is correct?
question: Two particles are projected from the same point with the same speed u such that they have the same range R, but different maximum heights, <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Which of the following is correct?
solution:
We get same range for angle of projections θ and  90-θ .
h=u2sin2θ2g
⇒h1=u2sin2θ2g,h2=u2cos2θ2g
h1h2=u4sin2θcos2θ4g2=4u4sin2θcos2θ16g2
=u2sin2θg2116=R216
∴    R2=16h1h2

subject: Physics
q: A swimmer wants to cross a river from point A to point B. Line AB makes an angle of 30° with the flow of the river. The magnitude of the velocity of the swimmer is the same as that of the river. The angle θ with the line AB should be _______°, so that the swimmer reaches point B.
question: A swimmer wants to cross a river from point A to point B. Line AB makes an angle of 30° with the flow of the river. The magnitude of the velocity of the swimmer is the same as that of the river. The angle θ with the line AB should be _______°, so that the swimmer reaches point B.
solution: Both velocity vectors are of same magnitude therefore resultant would pass exactly midway through them θ=30°

subject: Physics
q: A small bob tied at one end of a thin string of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is describing a vertical circle so that the maximum and minimum tension in the string is in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The velocity of the bob at the highest position is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A small bob tied at one end of a thin string of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is describing a vertical circle so that the maximum and minimum tension in the string is in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The velocity of the bob at the highest position is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Let the speed of bob at lowest position be v1 and at the highest position be v2.

Maximum tension is at lowest position and minimum tension is at the highest position.
Now, using, conservation of mechanical energy,

12mv12=12mv22+mg2l

⇒  v12=v22+4 gl   ....1

Now, Tmax-mg=mv12l

⇒ Tmax=mg+mv12l

&  Tmin+mg=mv22l

⇒ Tmin=mv22l-mg

TmaxTmin=51

⇒  mg+mv12lmv22l-mg=51

⇒ mg+mv12l=mv22l-mg5

⇒ mg+mlv22+4 gl=5mv22l-5mg

⇒ mg+mv22l+4mg=5mv22l-5mg

⇒ 10 mg=4mv22l

v2=10×10×14

Thus, the velocity of the bob at the highest position is 5 m s-1.

subject: Physics
q: A small block slides down from the top of hemisphere of radius R=3 m as shown in the figure. The height h at which the block will lose contact with the surface of the sphere is m. (Assume there is no friction between the block and the hemisphere)
question: A small block slides down from the top of hemisphere of radius R=3 m as shown in the figure. The height h at which the block will lose contact with the surface of the sphere is m. (Assume there is no friction between the block and the hemisphere)
solution:
mgcosθ=mv2R   ...(1)

cosθ=hR

Energy conservation

mg{R-h}=12mv2  ...(2)

 from (1) and (2) ⇒mghR=2mg{R-h}R

h=2R3=2 m

subject: Physics
q: A ball of mass 100 g is projected with velocity $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at $60^{\circ}$ with horizontal. The decrease in kinetic energy of the ball during the motion from point of projection to highest point is
question: A ball of mass 100 g is projected with velocity $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at $60^{\circ}$ with horizontal. The decrease in kinetic energy of the ball during the motion from point of projection to highest point is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{i}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2 \\ & \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\mathrm{v} \cos 60^{\circ}\right)^2=\frac{1}{8} m v^2 \\ & \Delta \mathrm{k}=\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{i}}-\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{3}{8} \mathrm{mv}^2=\frac{3}{8} \times 0.1 \times 400=15 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two objects are projected with same velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> however at different angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> with the horizontal. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>90</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, the ratio of horizontal range of the first object to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>2</mn><mi>nd</mi></msup></math> object will be :
question: Two objects are projected with same velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> however at different angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> with the horizontal. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>90</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, the ratio of horizontal range of the first object to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>2</mn><mi>nd</mi></msup></math> object will be :
solution:
Given: α+β=90°

Range of the first projectile:

R1=u2sin2αg

Range of the second projectile:

R2=u2sin2βg=u2sin(180°-2α)g=u2sin2αg

As we can see, both are equal.

R1=R2

Therefore,

R1:R2=1:1

subject: Physics
q: A passenger train of length <math><mn>60</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> travels at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>hr</mi></math>. Another freight train of length <math><mn>120</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> travels at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>hr</mi></math>. The ratio of times taken by the passenger train to completely cross the freight train when: (i) they are moving in the same direction, and (ii) in the opposite directions is:
question: A passenger train of length <math><mn>60</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> travels at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>hr</mi></math>. Another freight train of length <math><mn>120</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> travels at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>hr</mi></math>. The ratio of times taken by the passenger train to completely cross the freight train when: (i) they are moving in the same direction, and (ii) in the opposite directions is:
solution:
t1=260+12080-30

t2=260+12080+30

t1t2=115

subject: Physics
q: A ball is spun with angular acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>t</mi></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is in second and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, the ball has angular velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and angular position of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi></math>. The most appropriate expression for the angular position of the ball is
question: A ball is spun with angular acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>t</mi></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is in second and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, the ball has angular velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and angular position of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi></math>. The most appropriate expression for the angular position of the ball is
solution:
Given: α=6t2-2t

Using relation α=dωdt=6t2-2t

Integrating the above, 

∫10ωdω=∫0t6t2-2tdt

⇒ω-10=2t3-t2

Now, ω=dθdt=10+2t3-t2

∫4θdθ=∫0t10+2t3-t2dt

Integrating the above relation, 

θ-4=10t+t42-t33

Thus, angular position of the ball is θ=t42-t33+10t+4.

subject: Physics
q: Two projectiles are fired with same initial speed from same point on ground at angles of \(\left(45^{\circ}-\alpha\right)\) and \(\left(45^{\circ}+\alpha\right)\), respectively, with the horizontal direction. The ratio of their maximum heights attained is :
question: Two projectiles are fired with same initial speed from same point on ground at angles of \(\left(45^{\circ}-\alpha\right)\) and \(\left(45^{\circ}+\alpha\right)\), respectively, with the horizontal direction. The ratio of their maximum heights attained is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{Max}}=\frac{(\mathrm{u} \sin \theta)^2}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \\ & \frac{\left(\mathrm{H}_{\max }\right)_1}{\left(\mathrm{H}_{\max }\right)_2}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2(45-\alpha)}{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2(45+\alpha)} \\ & =\frac{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \alpha-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin \alpha\right)^2}{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \alpha+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin \alpha\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{1-\sin 2 \alpha}{1+\sin 2 \alpha}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A vehicle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving along a levelled curved road of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> with angular velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The centripetal force acting on the vehicle is:
question: A vehicle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving along a levelled curved road of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> with angular velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The centripetal force acting on the vehicle is:
solution:
The centripetal force is given by 

F=Mv2R=MRω2

The given data is 

M=200 kgR=70 mω=0.2 rad s-1

The magnitude of the force is 

F=200×70×0.22 N

⇒F=560 N

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving along a circular path with a constant speed of $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. What is the magnitude of the change in velocity of the particle, when it moves through an angle of $60^{\circ}$ around the centre of the circle?
question: A particle is moving along a circular path with a constant speed of $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. What is the magnitude of the change in velocity of the particle, when it moves through an angle of $60^{\circ}$ around the centre of the circle?
solution:
Change in velocity,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
|\Delta \overline{\mathrm{v}}|=\sqrt{\mathrm{v}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{v}_{2}^{2}+2 \mathrm{v}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{2} \cos (\pi-\theta)} \\
=2 \mathrm{v} \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \quad\left(\because\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{1}\right|=\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{2}\right|\right)=\mathrm{v} \\
=(2 \times 10) \times \sin \left(30^{\circ}\right)=2 \times 10 \times \frac{1}{2} \\
=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{array}
$$

subject: Physics
q: The initial speed of a bullet fired from a rifle is $630 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The rifle is fired at the centre of a target $700 \mathrm{~m}$ away at the same level as the target. How far above the centre of the target ?
question: The initial speed of a bullet fired from a rifle is $630 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The rifle is fired at the centre of a target $700 \mathrm{~m}$ away at the same level as the target. How far above the centre of the target ?

subject: Physics
q: Two bodies are projected from ground with same speeds <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at two different angles with respect to horizontal. The bodies were found to have same range. If one of the body was projected at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, with horizontal then sum of the maximum heights, attained by the two projectiles, is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: Two bodies are projected from ground with same speeds <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at two different angles with respect to horizontal. The bodies were found to have same range. If one of the body was projected at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, with horizontal then sum of the maximum heights, attained by the two projectiles, is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
For two projectiles with same speed to have the same horizontal range, angle of projection will be θ1+θ2=90°.

Therefore, θ2=90°-60°=30°.

Now, required sum of maximum heights will be,

Hmax1+Hmax2=u2sin2θ12g+u2sin2θ22g

=4022014+34=80 m

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> projected with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> making an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal. The magnitude of angular momentum of the projectile about the point of projection when the particle is at its maximum height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> is :
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> projected with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> making an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal. The magnitude of angular momentum of the projectile about the point of projection when the particle is at its maximum height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> is :
solution:
Angular momentum at maximum height will be, L=mucosθh, where h=maximum height

Therefore, L=mucosθ×u2sin2θ2g

=mu32g×32×122=3mu316g

subject: Physics
q: An object is projected with kinetic energy $K$ from a point $A$ at an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The ratio of the difference in kinetic energies at points $B$ and $C$ to that at point $A$ (see figure), in the absence of air friction is :
question: An object is projected with kinetic energy $K$ from a point $A$ at an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The ratio of the difference in kinetic energies at points $B$ and $C$ to that at point $A$ (see figure), in the absence of air friction is :
solution:
At A: $KE_A = K$, $v_A = v$, angle $= 60°$.
At B (highest point): $v_B = v\cos 60° = v/2$, so $KE_B = K/4$.
At C (same level as A): by energy conservation, $KE_C = K$.
$|KE_B - KE_C| = |K/4 - K| = 3K/4$.
Ratio $= \frac{3K/4}{K} = \frac{3}{4}$, i.e., $3:4$.
